segments.title: Tiger's hull makes a 15-inch refit physically plausible
segments.summary: Barbette diameters, beam, and displacement density turn the proposed 15-inch rearmament into a testable naval-architecture problem.
segments.body: This watch point starts the counterfactual with ship geometry rather than a simple wish for heavier guns. Tiger's 13.5-inch barbettes were about 24 feet 6 inches across, while the 15-inch Mark I mounting required roughly 30 feet, so the supporting structure needed to grow by about 2 feet 9 inches on every side. Dr. Clarke then compares Tiger's beam and displacement density with the Queen Elizabeth class, Renown, and Hood. Tiger scores much closer to ships already carrying 15-inch guns than to the more lightly built Renown. The calculation does not make the conversion easy, but it gives a concrete reason to regard the hull as strong and spacious enough for serious study.

evidence.note: Compares the 13.5-inch and 15-inch barbette diameters and calculates the extra width required.
Uses beam and displacement-density comparisons to argue that Tiger's hull could support the larger armament.

segments.title: Tiger approaches the fast battleship from the battlecruiser side
segments.summary: Tiger and the Queen Elizabeth class represent two British routes toward combining battle-line strength with high speed.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places Tiger on one side of a British design convergence toward the fast battleship. Battlecruisers develop toward Tiger while battleships develop toward the Queen Elizabeth class, producing ships that solve similar speed-and-protection problems from different starting points. Hood looks denser on a simple displacement calculation, but much of her protection was added after Jutland while she was already under construction. Tiger's internal structure was designed as a coherent, heavily built package from the outset. This makes the comparison useful for understanding why labels alone can conceal how close some late battlecruisers were to fast-battleship qualities.

evidence.note: Describes battlecruiser and battleship development as two British routes toward the fast battleship.
Contrasts Tiger's purpose-built density with protection added to Hood after Jutland.

segments.title: Speed, stability, and foreign battlecruisers explain Tiger's design
segments.summary: Trials speed, boiler pressure, metacentric height, and the Kongo-class challenge show why Tiger was more than a slightly enlarged Queen Mary.
segments.body: Tiger's official speed was 28 knots, but her fully laden trials reached about 29.5 knots despite coal-and-oil firing and a curious arrangement of 39 boilers. Dr. Clarke argues that full oil firing could plausibly have pushed her toward 30 knots. A strong metacentric height also made her a notably stable gun platform. Although only slightly longer and wider than Queen Mary, Tiger was roughly 2,000 tons heavier and represented a materially different design. British observation of German construction, Japan's Kongo-class orders, and faster battle-line concepts all increased the likelihood of battles between fast heavy ships and help explain the extra investment.

evidence.note: Covers trials speed, boiler pressure, oil firing, the 39-boiler layout, and metacentric stability.
Compares Tiger with Queen Mary and links the design to German ships, Japan's Kongo class, and rising battle-line speeds.

segments.title: Jutland supplies combat evidence for Tiger's resilience
segments.summary: Tiger's heavy shell damage and rapid return to service provide a wartime test of the hull strength assumed by the refit scenario.
segments.body: The Battle of Jutland turns Tiger's strength from a design claim into operational evidence. Dr. Clarke lists hits from fifteen 11-inch shells, at least two 12-inch shells, and several 5.9-inch rounds. Damage affected her belt area, flare stores, sick bay, cable spaces, and a funnel, yet the ship survived and returned to service quickly. That record does not prove that every reconstruction choice would work, but it does show a resilient structure capable of absorbing severe punishment. The example helps explain why the Royal Navy still valued Tiger when treaty negotiations began.

evidence.note: Enumerates Tiger's shell hits and damaged spaces at Jutland.
Emphasizes the ship's survival, resilience, and rapid return to service.

segments.title: Washington treaty rules block the historical Tiger gun change
segments.summary: Tiger fits the treaty's broad tonnage and caliber ceilings, but the reconstruction clause forbids changing her main-gun caliber or mounting type.
segments.body: This section separates what was technically possible from what the Washington Naval Treaty allowed. The reconstruction rules permitted bulges, blisters, anti-aircraft protection, and up to 3,000 tons of defensive additions. They barred changes to side armor and to the caliber, number, or general mounting type of a retained capital ship's main armament, except for specified French, Italian, and British cases. Tiger would remain below the 35,000-ton standard-displacement ceiling and the 16-inch gun limit even after a substantial rebuild. Her problem was therefore not the broad size of the resulting ship but the specific ban on changing the main battery, which explains why the conversion did not happen historically.

evidence.note: Reads the treaty's defensive-reconstruction allowance and prohibition on main-armament changes.
Explains that Tiger met the overall tonnage and caliber limits but failed the reconstruction rule.

segments.title: Stored Courageous turrets expose Britain's deliberate rearmament pace
segments.summary: Complete 15-inch mountings from the Courageous-class conversions supplied a practical hardware source and complicate claims of panicked British rearmament.
segments.body: Britain did not need to design every part of a Tiger rearmament from scratch. The 15-inch guns and turrets removed during the Courageous-class carrier conversions were stored, leaving the main hardware for an eight-gun ship available. Dr. Clarke connects this stockpile with Britain's broader reserves of armor plate and willingness to buy specialist material abroad. If political leaders had believed that emergency rearmament justified any expense, they could have used these assets to pursue a Vanguard-like capital ship much earlier. Their decision not to do so supports his interpretation of a measured political and economic response rather than uncontrolled panic.

evidence.note: Identifies the stored Courageous-class turrets and explains why hull strength matters when carrying armor.
Uses stored guns and armor-supply options to characterize British rearmament as politically measured.

segments.title: Tiger's strategic speed could put heavy guns at Singapore in time
segments.summary: Transit-time arithmetic shows why fast capital ships served as a deterrent and rapid-response force for Britain's Far Eastern commitments.
segments.body: Tiger's 1918 refit and continued retention make sense when viewed through the Royal Navy's worldwide response problem. At a crisis cruising speed near 24 knots, a fast squadron could reach Singapore from Portsmouth in about fourteen days or from Malta in roughly ten. Dr. Clarke compares those times with a possible Japanese movement from Sasebo, including warning from mobilization before the assault force sailed. A fast British force could therefore arrive while the crisis was still developing, whereas an 18-to-21-knot battle fleet might be too late. The aim was not necessarily to fight immediately but to place enough visible power in the region to deter escalation until the larger fleet followed.

evidence.note: Introduces Tiger's 1918 refit and compares fast-squadron transit times from Portsmouth and Malta with the battle fleet.
Relates Japanese mobilization warning to Singapore timing and describes the fast ships as a deterrent rapid-response force.

segments.title: The London treaty removes Tiger before her replacement is ready
segments.summary: Tiger left service in 1931 as treaty tonnage was cleared for a new generation, but delays postponed the King George V class and left a capability gap.
segments.body: Tiger remained in commission until 1931 under her final captain, Arthur Bedford. Dr. Clarke links her withdrawal both to the London Naval Treaty and to British plans to free tonnage for the next generation of capital ships. The replacement logic did not produce an immediate successor because the future program suffered repeated delays before the King George V class began. Tiger was sold for breaking in 1932, although accounts differ on when physical dismantling started. This short passage matters because it shows how treaty accounting could remove an existing capability well before a planned replacement reached the fleet.

evidence.note: Connects Tiger's 1931 withdrawal with the London treaty, replacement tonnage, and delays to the King George V class.
Dates the sale for breaking and notes uncertainty over when dismantling actually began.

segments.title: Third Sea Lords connect Tiger to worldwide task-group operations
segments.summary: Frederick Field, Ernle Chatfield, and Roger Backhouse supply the institutional case for fast capital ships able to reinforce dispersed forces.
segments.body: The alternate-history mechanism begins with the Third Sea Lords who could turn strategic need into ship-design policy. Frederick Field held the post long enough to develop a worldwide vision and favored the fast F3 concept over the slower N3 because a future war might be fought far from Britain. Dr. Clarke argues that an ideal force arriving late is operationally useless, while permanent forward deployment would require far more ships and money. Battlecruisers were an interim answer until machinery achieved the power density needed for a fully armored fast battleship. Ernle Chatfield and Roger Backhouse also appear as officers thinking about dispersed task groups that would need strategic speed to maneuver and support one another across the globe.

evidence.note: Introduces the Third Sea Lords, Field's preference for F3, global force projection, and the machinery tradeoff behind battlecruisers.
Discusses Chatfield, Backhouse, dispersed task groups, mutual support, and strategic speed.

segments.title: A 15-inch standardization bargain changes the treaty path
segments.summary: Britain could seek either an exemption for work already begun or a treaty bargain that traded 16-inch prestige for 15-inch fleet commonality.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke proposes two political routes around the historical restriction. Britain could claim that Tiger's conversion was already under way and seek treatment similar to Renown, or it could give up 16-inch ambitions in exchange for standardizing future capital ships on the 15-inch gun. The latter choice would simplify fleet logistics while surrendering little practical striking power. The treaty's separate 3,000-ton defensive allowance could then make the rebuilt ship even more substantial. Tiger's declared 28,500-ton standard displacement excludes thousands of tons of fuel and water, so the discussion also demonstrates why standard and full-load figures cannot be used interchangeably when judging a capital ship's real size.

evidence.note: Sets out the 15-inch standardization bargain and the alternative claim that reconstruction was already in progress.
Explains the 3,000-ton allowance, fuel and water exclusions, and Tiger's size relative to other British capital ships.

segments.title: New boilers turn Tiger's refit into a machinery transformation
segments.summary: Oil firing and Nelson-era boilers could replace Tiger's 39-boiler plant with a much smaller, more powerful arrangement while freeing internal and deck space.
segments.body: The proposed rebuild changes far more than the gun mountings. Converting Tiger to all-oil firing would release coal-bunker space, while new boilers and possibly geared turbines would permit a major rearrangement of the machinery rooms and funnels. Dr. Clarke compares Tiger's 39 older water-tube boilers, which produced 85,000 shaft horsepower, with the eight higher-pressure Admiralty three-drum boilers used for Nelson and Rodney's 45,000 horsepower. On that rough power-density basis, about sixteen modern boilers could give Tiger roughly 90,000 horsepower while occupying far less space. The result might sustain about 29.5 knots or approach 30, improve the secondary-gun layout, and make the modernization a whole-ship engineering project rather than a turret exchange.

evidence.note: Sketches the funnel, oil-firing, bunker, secondary-gun, turbine, and boiler changes.
Compares Tiger's 39 boilers with Nelson and Rodney's plant and estimates the rebuilt ship's power and speed.

segments.title: Armor coverage, not only maximum thickness, shapes the rebuild
segments.summary: Tiger's 9-inch belt and original structural integration offer a different protection tradeoff from Hood's thicker but later-added armor.
segments.body: Tiger carried a 9-inch belt, about half again as thick as the principal protection on Renown and Repulse but below Hood's 12-inch maximum. Dr. Clarke cautions that maximum thickness alone does not settle which ship had the better scheme because Tiger's belt covered a different proportion of the hull. Her armor was integrated into the design from the beginning, whereas much of Hood's additional protection entered after construction had started. Tiger also had up to 3 inches of deck armor, and her existing 8-to-9-inch barbettes would be replaced as part of the main-gun conversion. The final protection package would therefore depend on whether the political route allowed only an agreed pre-treaty rebuild or secured broader concessions during negotiations.

evidence.note: Compares Tiger's belt, armor coverage, deck armor, and barbettes with Renown, Repulse, and Hood.
Links the achievable protection changes to the two proposed treaty routes.

segments.title: Rebuilding Tiger buys time and completes a four-ship fast force
segments.summary: A 1918 reconstruction could bridge the gap to new designs and give the Royal Navy four fast capital ships for a sustainable deploy-train-refit cycle.
segments.body: In the first scenario, Tiger's reconstruction begins in 1918 to preserve a useful fast capital ship while new Admirals or successor designs remain unsettled. The Royal Navy wanted Courageous and Glorious out of the battle line quickly, but Tiger was its newest purpose-built battlecruiser and had spent most of the war operating near home maintenance facilities. Dr. Clarke compares her age with Warspite to show that hard wartime service did not automatically make a major rebuild unreasonable. Retaining Tiger alongside Hood, Renown, and Repulse would create a four-ship fast force, enough in a standard force-generation model to keep one deployed, one training or transiting, one in light work, and one in major refit. Oil firing could also improve range, while eight 15-inch guns would give Tiger status and combat weight beyond the six-gun Renown and Repulse.

evidence.note: Frames the refit as a bridge to future ships and compares Tiger's age and service condition with Warspite.
Explains four-hull force generation, the resulting gun balance, improved endurance, prestige use, and possible bow changes.

segments.title: Accepting Tiger may be cheaper than reopening the treaty bargain
segments.summary: Treaty partners might tolerate a conversion already under way rather than risk British withdrawal or a demand for another new capital ship.
segments.body: The treaty system often accepted work and ships that already existed, as the special treatment of Renown and Hood illustrates. Dr. Clarke argues that refusing Tiger could prompt Britain either to leave the agreement or to demand authority for a third new 16-inch capital ship, worsening the tonnage arithmetic for other powers. Allowing one more 15-inch reconstruction could therefore become the least disruptive compromise. The long-term effect is more important than simply retaining Tiger: Britain enters the Second World War with four fast 15-inch capital ships. Tiger can cover assignments that historically fell to Hood, making it easier to send Hood into her planned modernization and changing which assets are available when war begins.

evidence.note: Explains treaty acceptance of the status quo and the alternatives Britain could demand if Tiger were refused.
Traces the effect of a fourth fast ship on Tiger's employment and Hood's modernization schedule.

segments.title: Treaty tonnage could produce three or four F3-style capital ships
segments.summary: Giving up 16-inch guns could let Britain use its remaining treaty allowance for a larger family of fast 15-inch ships derived from F3 thinking.
segments.body: The more ambitious scenario treats 15-inch standardization as a bargaining asset rather than merely an exemption for Tiger. After removing the four 13.5-inch battleships, Britain retained a little over 136,000 tons of capital-ship allowance. That was enough on paper for four ships near 34,000 tons, although Dr. Clarke judges that three would be a more likely negotiated maximum. Even three F3-style ships, added to four battlecruisers, five Queen Elizabeths, and five R-class battleships, would create an unusually large and fast capital fleet. Tiger might also serve as a test platform for a 15-inch/50-caliber weapon, while the F2 and F3 concepts continue the heavily protected, battlecruiser-derived route toward the fast battleship.

evidence.note: Calculates the remaining British treaty allowance and the possible three- or four-ship F3 program.
Suggests Tiger as a 15-inch/50 test ship and describes F2/F3 as a continuation of battlecruiser development toward the fast battleship.

segments.title: Two battle lines give Britain worldwide task-group depth
segments.summary: Seven or eight fast capital ships create a second operational battle line with enough depth for simultaneous deployment, maintenance, and deterrence.
segments.body: A fleet with four F3s and four older battlecruisers would possess something close to two distinct battle lines. The conventional battle fleet could hold concentrated positions while fast ships formed task groups able to move between theaters. Dr. Clarke emphasizes availability as much as headline totals: the larger pool makes it realistic to have several fast capital ships at sea and several more ready in port despite training and refit demands. Even the less expansive seven-ship version would generate substantial deployable strength. The difference between 15-inch and 16-inch guns matters less in this argument than the number of heavy, fast hulls an opponent must account for.

evidence.note: Describes two battle lines and the Royal Navy's capacity to generate worldwide task groups.
Connects a seven- or eight-ship fast force with availability, deterrence, and the limited practical difference between 15-inch and 16-inch hits.

segments.title: A larger fast fleet undermines the German Panzerschiff concept
segments.summary: More British ships able to catch and outgun Panzerschiffe change both Royal Navy training and Germany's incentive to build surface raiders.
segments.body: Extra fast capital ships would give the Royal Navy more opportunities to modernize older units and to exercise against realistic high-speed raiders. Training forces could practice finding a freely maneuvering capital ship rather than relying only on abstract fleet problems. The strategic effect falls even more heavily on Germany. Panzerschiffe already faced serious risk from Hood, Renown, and Repulse; adding Tiger and several fast Nelson- or F3-derived ships greatly expands the number of hunters able to catch and outclass them. Dr. Clarke therefore suggests that German planners might abandon or substantially alter the surface-raider path before the Second World War.

evidence.note: Links a deeper force to more refits and exercises against high-speed surface raiders.
Argues that numerous fast British capital ships make Panzerschiffe less viable and could redirect German development.

segments.title: Fast battleships alter Mediterranean operations, Denmark Strait, and carrier design
segments.summary: A larger pool of 28-knot capital ships changes where Britain can accept risk and forces carriers to support a faster operational formation.
segments.body: Faster Nelson-derived ships need not spend as much time guarding fixed choke points such as Gibraltar. With more high-speed capital ships available, British commanders could accept greater operational risk and bring heavier forces to battles such as Matapan. A 15-inch standard might also reshape the later King George V class, potentially avoiding some difficulties associated with the historical quadruple turrets. Greater availability could produce a different force at the Denmark Strait and other operations where strategic speed determined which ships could participate. Carriers would then face a design requirement to keep pace with a formation progressing at roughly 24 knots rather than an 18-knot battle line, linking the battleship counterfactual directly to naval aviation.

evidence.note: Discusses Gibraltar, Matapan, possible King George V armament changes, Denmark Strait, and strategic speed.
Explains why carrier designs would need to keep pace with the faster capital-ship force.

segments.title: An eighteenth capital ship could support an Eastern battle squadron
segments.summary: Ceylon or western India offers a politically Eastern but operationally flexible base for the extra squadron created by a larger treaty fleet.
segments.body: The closing force-structure calculation asks where Britain would place an additional battle squadron if the fleet reached eighteen capital ships. Direct basing at Singapore is less attractive once Fascist Italy and Mediterranean tension demand flexibility closer to the Suez route. Dr. Clarke proposes Trincomalee in Ceylon or a base on India's west coast as alternatives. Either would signal a visible commitment to Australia, New Zealand, and Britain's Eastern possessions. At the same time, ships stationed there could move through the Arabian Sea, Red Sea, and Suez Canal to reinforce the Mediterranean from an unexpected direction, turning basing geography into a bridge between imperial defense and European war planning.

evidence.note: Calculates the eighteen-ship fleet and proposes forming another battle squadron.
Considers Trincomalee or western India as bases able to support both Eastern commitments and rapid movement toward the Mediterranean.
